channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Neanderthal Dentistry, and the Scientist Glad Not to Have Experienced It
item.description: The prehistoric hominins “apparently were very adept at what we would consider invasive medicine,” said the anthropologist John Olsen.
item.dc:creator: Franz Lidz

item.title: Military Bases Are Rife With ‘Forever Chemicals.’ New Mexico Wants Them Cleaned Up.
item.description: The state is leading the country’s reckoning with PFAS. The outcome of its suit against the federal government will affect how courts treat more than 15,000 other claims nationwide.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Nazaryan and Nina Riggio
item.media:description: Art Schaap, right, a dairy farmer in Clovis, N.M., and James Kenney, of the New Mexico Environment Department, stand in the area where Mr. Schaap had to kill his cattle.

item.title: A 16th-Century Sketch of Anne Boleyn More Likely Depicted Her Mother, an A.I. Analysis Finds
item.description: Using facial-recognition technology, scholars have concluded that a 500-year-old drawing labeled “Anna Bollein Queen” more likely showed her mother, Elizabeth Howard.
item.dc:creator: Katherine Kornei
item.media:credit: Royal Collection Enterprises Limited 2025/Royal Collection Trust

item.title: She Was Finding Sources of Dangerous Water and Soil Pollution
item.description: Melanie Malone led a research project to identify and study contamination sites in Washington State. Then the E.P.A. canceled her grant.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:credit: Meron Tekie Menghistab for The New York Times

item.title: How a Funding Pause Derailed an Artificial Heart for Babies
item.description: James Antaki’s efforts to develop a baby’s heart were close to success when his federal funding was cut off. The grants were eventually restored; rebuilding what was lost wasn’t so easy.
item.dc:creator: Simar Bajaj
item.media:credit: Celia Talbot Tobin for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Antaki, a biomedical engineer at Cornell. He had been developing an artificial heart for young children. Roughly 14,000 children and babies are hospitalized with heart failure each year in the United States.

item.title: Archaeologists Find Egyptian Mummy Buried With the ‘Iliad’
item.description: For the deceased of Roman-era Egypt, Greek literature may have offered a cheat code to a more comfortable afterlife.
item.dc:creator: Franz Lidz
item.media:credit: Maite Mascort i Roca and Esther Pons Mellado, Mission of the University of Barcelona at Oxyrhynchus
item.media:description: The necropolis of Oxyrhynchus in Egypt, where a nonroyal mummy was found with some lines on papyrus from Book 2 of Homer’s “Iliad.”

item.title: Can Some Very Tiny Particles Cool the Planet? One Tech Company Says Yes.
item.description: Stardust Solutions says its tiny spheres can reflect the sun’s rays without harming people or the environment. Critics say private companies have no business altering Earth’s atmosphere.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:credit: Avishag Shaar-Yashuv for The New York Times

item.title: A Physicist Who Thinks in Poetry from the Cosmic Edge
item.description: In her second pop-science book, theoretical cosmologist Chanda Prescod-Weinstein returns to her celestial and cultural roots.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.category: The Edge of Space-Time: Particles, Poetry, and the Cosmic Dream Boogie (Book)
The Disordered Cosmos: A Journey into Dark Matter, Spacetime, and Dreams Deferred (Book)
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times

item.title: Building Nests Is Hard. That’s Why Some Birds Steal.
item.description: When researchers used GPS devices to monitor the nests of Hawaiian honeycreepers, they found dozens of cases of brazen avian burglary.
item.dc:creator: Sara Novak

item.title: Fish and Wildlife Service Clears a Weedkiller, Saying It Won’t Cause Extinction
item.description: The finding effectively paves the way for continued use of atrazine, a widely used herbicide that has been linked to birth defects and cancer in humans.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:description: Test plots at a Syngenta research site in Junction City, Kan. Atrazine is made primarily by Syngenta.

item.title: Scientists Tweaked the Global Warming Outlook. So Trump Weighed In.
item.description: Renewable energy has helped make the worst-case scenario a bit less bad. The president said, falsely, it shows that climate scientists were wrong all along.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:description: Solar panels and wind turbines in north-central China. Renewable is becoming more affordable in many countries.

item.title: E.P.A. to Repeal Some Limits on ‘Forever Chemicals’ in Drinking Water
item.description: The rules were established by the Biden administration after research linked the compounds to a range of serious health problems.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:description: Lee Zeldin, the E.P.A. administrator, has argued that the agency made a procedural error when it established the drinking water standards in 2024.

item.title: Ebola Was Identified in Congo Weeks Before W.H.O. Declared an Emergency
item.description: Early surveillance and testing failed to identify the rare species of Ebola responsible for the current outbreak. An American doctor is among the confirmed cases.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean
item.category: Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:description: At a hospital in Ituri Province, Democratic Republic of Congo, the center of a new Ebola outbreak.

item.title: Children’s Mental Health Visits Have Shot Up, Research Shows
item.description: Doctor’s visits for children’s anxiety rose by more than 250 percent over 10 years, according to a study of nearly two million children.
item.dc:creator: Ellen Barry
item.media:credit: Annie Flanagan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Visits were counted as mental health visits if a diagnostic code was included in the claim, either because the child or the family raised the issue or because the child screened positive for mental health symptoms during the visit.

item.title: When Tornado Weather Hits, These Scientists Break Out the Colored Pencils
item.description: With a battery of modern technology at their fingertips, meteorologists often turn first to an old-fashioned tracking technique.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones and Desiree Rios

item.title: The Quest for Clean Hydrogen Moves Underground
item.description: The dream of clean hydrogen has tantalized energy experts for years, but producing it has been tough. Many start-ups think the answer could lie beneath our feet.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer and Ian Willms

item.title: How a ‘Model’ for Climate Migration Became a Cautionary Tale
item.description: The residents of Isle de Jean Charles in Louisiana found safety after moving to higher ground. But the experience left some of them warning others facing relocation: ‘Don’t do it.’
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance and Wayan Barre
item.media:credit: Wayan Barre for The New York Times

item.title: Michigan Battles Trump Over His Order to Keep an Old Coal Plant Running
item.description: The Trump administration broke the law, Michigan and others told a court, by declaring an “energy emergency” and forcing an aging coal-burning plant to keep operating.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Jim West/UCG/Universal Images Group, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The J.H. Campbell coal-burning power plant in West Olive, Mich. The state is challenging a Trump order to keep the aging plant active.

item.title: 16 More People in the U.S. Are Being Monitored for Hantavirus, C.D.C. Says
item.description: They were passengers on a plane to Johannesburg with an infected Dutch woman who later died.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: A person in a biohazard suit at the airport in Omaha, Neb., as passengers evacuated from the MV Hondius arrived on Monday.

item.title: Hantavirus Doesn’t Spread Easily, but Officials May Be Downplaying Risks
item.description: The virus is clearly far less contagious than the coronavirus, scientists agree, but they have found cases where it spread among people without direct contact.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Passengers from the MV Hondius evacuated from the cruise ship this week in Tenerife, Spain.

item.title: Will Her Daughter Be Safe at Pali High as It Rebuilds From LA Wildfires?
item.description: It’s a nightmare faced by families all around Los Angeles: After wildfire smoke blanketed homes, schools and offices with toxic chemicals, when is it OK to go back?
item.dc:creator: Emily Baumgaertner Nunn and Jenna Schoenefeld
item.category: Palisades Charter High School (Pacific Palisades, Calif)
item.media:credit: Jenna Schoenefeld for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pearl Abrahams, a senior at Palisades Charter High School in California, attended classes — along with 2,400 other students — in an abandoned department store while her school, burned in the Palisades Fire last year, was rehabilitated.

item.title: Black Bear Fatally Mauls Uranium Contractor in Northern Canada
item.description: The attack, at a remote uranium mining site in northern Saskatchewan, was only the fourth fatal black bear encounter in the province’s recorded history, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:description: The black bear is the most common species in Canada, though bear attacks are rare.

item.title: Nancy Cox, Who Worked to Conquer the Wily Flu, Dies at 77
item.description: As the leader of the C.D.C.’s influenza division, she battled to keep up with an ever-changing viral opponent, building a global network of researchers and forecasters.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

item.title: Rich Danker, RFK Jr.’s Top Spokesman, Resigns in Protest Over New Vaping Policy
item.description: In a letter to President Trump, the spokesman, Rich Danker, said allowing the sale of flavored e-cigarettes would enhance their appeal to children.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.media:credit: Michael A. McCoy for The New York Times
item.media:description: The resignation comes at a fraught time for the Department of Health and Human Services, which now has three major vacancies.

item.title: With a Friend in Trump, the Tobacco Industry Secures a Lucrative Win
item.description: In a dispute over vapes, the president sided with tobacco companies that filled his groups’ coffers over his own F.D.A. commissioner, who resigned in protest.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett and Kenneth P. Vogel
item.media:description: The F.D.A. issued new guidance that could pave the way for major tobacco companies to begin selling flavored vapes less than a week after industry executives met with President Trump.

item.title: 5 Great Stargazing Trains
item.description: On nighttime excursions in dark-sky hot spots like Norway, New Zealand and Nevada, all you have to do is relax and look up. The stars will do the rest.
item.dc:creator: Michael Harmon

item.title: Lithuania’s Peat Bogs Could Help the Climate and Defend the Border, Too
item.description: Lithuanian officials hope restored peat bogs can reinforce the border in addition to locking away planet-warming carbon.
item.dc:creator: Avril Silva
